data.title: LayerZero Investigation Report: Analysis of the Direct Causes and Process of the KelpDAO Hack
data.description:
Source: LayerZero; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

KelpDAO Attack Statement

On April 18, 2026, KelpDAO was attacked, resulting in a loss of approximately $290 million. Initial indications suggest that this attack originated from a highly sophisticated state-sponsored hacking group, most likely North Korea's Lazarus Group (specifically a branch of TraderTraitor). This incident was limited to KelpDAO's rsETH configuration, and its direct cause was its use of a single DVN (Decentralized Validator Network) setup. No risk contagion was observed in other cross-chain assets or applications. This highly sophisticated attack targeted the poisoning of the downstream RPC (Remote Procedure Call) infrastructure used by LayerZero Labs DVN. Currently, all affected RPC nodes have been deprecated and replaced, and LayerZero Labs DVN is now back online. We share these details to help the community better understand and defend against this emerging state-sponsored attack vector. Background: LayerZero's Modular Security Architecture The LayerZero protocol is built on a modular, application-configurable security foundation. Decentralized Validator Networks (DVNs) are independent entities responsible for verifying the integrity of cross-chain messages. Crucially, the protocol does not mandate a single security configuration. Instead, it empowers each application and asset issuer to define their own security posture, including which DVNs they rely on, how they combine them, and what redundancy thresholds they set. Industry best practice—and what LayerZero explicitly recommends to all integrators—is to configure a diverse and redundant multi-DVN setup. This means that no single DVN should represent a single point of trust or failure. Scope and Contagion Situation: rsETH Only We have conducted a comprehensive review of the active integration on the LayerZero protocol. We can confidently confirm that there is no risk contagion to any other asset or application. This incident was entirely isolated from KelpDAO's rsETH configuration due to its single DVN setup. The affected application was rsETH, issued by KelpDAO. At the time of the incident, its OApp configuration relied on a "1/1" DVN setup, with LayerZero Labs as the sole validator—a configuration that directly violated LayerZero's long-standing recommendation of a multi-DVN redundancy model to all partners. Running a single point-of-failure configuration meant there was no independent validator to catch and reject forged messages. LayerZero and other external organizations had previously conveyed best practices regarding DVN diversification to KelpDAO, but despite these recommendations, KelpDAO chose to use a 1/1 DVN configuration.

With proper hardening configuration, an attack would require consensus to be reached across multiple independent DVNs, rendering the attack ineffective if any single DVN is compromised.

Event Summary

On April 18, 2026, LayerZero Labs' DVN became the target of a highly sophisticated attack. The attacker compromised the quorum RPCs that DVN relies on for transaction verification by tampering with or "poisoning" the downstream RPC infrastructure. This was not achieved through a protocol vulnerability, DVN itself, or a key management vulnerability.

Instead, the attacker obtained a list of RPCs used by our DVN, compromised two separate nodes, and replaced the binary running the op-geth node. Due to our "least privilege" principle, they were unable to compromise the actual DVN instance.

However, they used this as a springboard to execute an RPC spoofing attack: A malicious node used a custom payload to forge messages to DVN. The node lied to DVN but reported the truth to any other IP address, including our scanning service and internal monitoring infrastructure. This was carefully designed to prevent security monitoring from detecting the anomaly. After the attack was complete, the malicious node self-destructed, disabling RPC and deleting the malicious binary and related logs. Furthermore, the attackers also launched a DDoS attack on the uncompromised RPC, triggering a failover to the poisoned RPC node. As a result, the DVN instance operated by LayerZero Labs confirmed transactions that never actually occurred. LayerZero Labs' Security Posture We run a complete Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) system, strict access control, a fully isolated environment, and full system logging. Our DVN runs across in-house and external RPC nodes. We are currently in the final stages of our SOC2 audit. The Road Ahead DVN Recovery: LayerZero Labs DVN has recovered. Applications using multi-DVN configurations can safely resume operations. Forced Migration: We are contacting all applications using a 1/1 DVN configuration, requiring them to migrate to a multi-DVN redundancy configuration. LayerZero Labs DVN will no longer sign or certify messages for any applications using a 1/1 configuration. Law Enforcement Cooperation: We are working with multiple law enforcement agencies worldwide and supporting industry partners and Seal911 in tracking funds.

Summary

We want to be clear on one point: the LayerZero protocol itself functioned exactly as expected throughout the entire incident. No protocol vulnerabilities were found. If this were a monolithic or shared security system, risk contagion could spread to all applications. The only defining characteristic of the LayerZero architecture is modular security, which played its due role in this case—completely isolating the attack within a single application, with zero risk of contagion within the system.

We will continue to dedicate ourselves to the security and integrity of the LayerZero ecosystem.

data.title: The US-Iran drama is so dramatic, even a screenwriter wouldn't dare write it like this.
data.description:
(Part 1)

It's not that we don't understand, it's that the world is changing too fast.

This weekend, a reversal every day, the US-Iran high-speed chase drama, so fast that even screenwriters probably wouldn't dare to write it like this.

Major national affairs, war and peace, can be so dramatic.

Military and national affairs, war and peace, can be so dramatic.

Just as the US and Iran were locked in fierce conflict, and just as Trump threatened to no longer be a "good guy," he suddenly announced that a US delegation would be going to Pakistan for negotiations. Just when the world thought a second round of talks was imminent, Iran refused to participate. It turned out the two sides hadn't reached an agreement; this was merely wishful thinking on the part of the US. Even more surprisingly, since talks were imminent, shouldn't some positive atmosphere have been created? Instead, the US military opened fire on an Iranian cargo ship at this crucial moment… This made the situation even more unpredictable. Let's briefly summarize. Leaving aside the previous struggles, let's start with the developments in the Strait of Hormuz over the past three days. One twist after another. Every scene in this drama is unpredictable. The first twist: the opening and closing of the Strait of Hormuz. Iranian Foreign Minister Araqchi announced on the 17th that, given the ceasefire reached between Lebanon and Israel, Iran would open the Strait of Hormuz to all merchant ships during the ceasefire period. Trump immediately responded, thanking Iran and revealing various "secrets," such as Iran agreeing never to use the Strait of Hormuz as a weapon, but the US would continue its maritime blockade, and that the US would enter Iran "at a leisurely pace" to take away Iran's enriched uranium "without spending a penny." Iran was furious. Iranian Parliament Speaker Ghalibaf subsequently clarified that the seven points made by the US president within an hour were all nonsense. Iran then announced that, based on the US's "repeated violations of commitments," it was restoring control of the Strait of Hormuz. On the 18th, Iran also fired on two Indian cargo ships. Seeing the situation was unfavorable, a large number of oil tankers waiting to cross the Strait turned back. India urgently summoned the Iranian ambassador, raising serious concerns and demanding an explanation from Iran. The good news lasted only a day. Iran, facing Trump, could probably only sigh: "I offered my heart to the bright moon, but the bright moon shone on the gutter." The second twist: whether Vance would go or not. Trump was furious, but declared that the US-Iran dialogue was "progressing well," saying that Tehran "could not blackmail the US." He then announced that the US delegation would travel to Pakistan for negotiations, but due to security concerns, Vice President Vance would not participate; Middle East Special Envoy Witkov and Trump's son-in-law Kushner would attend. However, the US quickly clarified that Vice President Vance would participate, and the negotiations would be held in Pakistan on Tuesday. Trump, you, a president, don't even know your vice president's schedule. The arrogance and incompetence of power often begin with confusion about information. The third twist: to talk or not to talk? Trump declared that talks would begin immediately and that a US delegation was on its way; however, he also threatened that he was no longer a peacemaker and that if Iran did not accept the agreement, the US would destroy every power plant and bridge in Iran. Iran, in turn, stated that it had not yet decided to participate in the talks and that the US must first lift the naval blockade. The Pakistani Prime Minister specifically called the Iranian President, presumably to offer various earnest persuasion. At this critical moment, the US pulled another stunt: Trump announced a standoff with an Iranian cargo ship, during which US forces opened fire and took control of the vessel. Iran claimed that the Revolutionary Guard Navy arrived in time, forcing US forces to retreat and flee the area. Iran also launched multiple drones to strike several US warships. Is this creating a favorable atmosphere for negotiations? Next, it's Iran's turn to retaliate.

（二）

Why are there so many plot twists?

I don't know either. The whole world is probably stunned. I've seen dramatic things, but I've never seen anything this dramatic. Every day is a climax, every day there's a plot twist, every day is wonderful. ...>

However, everyone should pay close attention to the timing. On Monday, April 20th, global stock markets will open and international oil markets will begin trading. Because of the Iranian Foreign Minister's statement that the Strait of Hormuz is "fully open," and even more so because of Trump's extremely optimistic and suggestive remarks, the previous Friday (April 17th), the US stock market surged and international oil prices plummeted. Americans were jubilant, and Trump was extremely pleased. As I analyzed in a previous article, this was a devastating blow to Iran. Simply put, the most intense battleground between the US and Iran right now isn't on land or at sea, but in the capital markets. Iran understands very well that the most important reason for Trump's TACO (Trump's tendency to back down) is the plummeting stock prices and soaring oil prices. The more this situation arises, the greater the pressure on Trump, and the more advantageous it is for Iran in negotiations. However, Trump used this as an excuse on Friday, with various details causing the stock market to surge and oil prices to plummet, which is extremely detrimental to Iran. Especially Iran's announcement of opening the Strait of Hormuz, while Trump declared that the blockade would remain in place—this is both a public slap in the face to Iran and the loss of its most important bargaining chip. So Iran quickly changed course, reinstating its blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and even attacking Indian cargo ships, clearly demonstrating to the world that Iran still controls the strait. If nothing unexpected happens, global stock markets on Monday will inevitably experience another retaliatory plunge, leaving Americans heartbroken. This is clearly what Trump least wants to see. Therefore, announcing good news about the negotiations before Monday's market opened, and immediately sending a US delegation to Pakistan, was an attempt to stabilize investor confidence and prevent the hard-won situation from being completely lost. Iran, of course, saw through this, which is why it has been hesitant to comment on whether it will participate in the new round of talks, keeping the market anxious and putting even more pressure on Trump. Furthermore, it must be said that the world is truly a giant makeshift operation. The President of the United States, having just announced that the Vice President would not go to Islamabad for negotiations, then clarified that the Vice President would still go. Is the President unable to prevail over the Vice President, or is the Vice President willing to contradict the President? We don't know. However, if Vance doesn't go, given Iran's animosity towards Witkov and Kushner, the negotiations are likely to break down; and even if they do succeed, would Vance be willing to let others reap the rewards? But just as the negotiations were about to begin, at a crucial moment, the US military created another obstacle by firing on and seizing an Iranian cargo ship. This could be an implementation of Trump's maximum pressure campaign, but it's also possible it's a reckless act born of a history of domineering behavior. Is this provoking a more violent retaliation from Iran? The US stock market must be in dire straits again. While Trump appears tough on the surface, he's probably cursing behind the scenes, calling them a bunch of useless bastards. Finally, how should we view this? In my personal opinion, here are three basic points: First, Trump is currently very anxious. If he weren't anxious, he wouldn't be making contradictory statements; if he weren't anxious, he wouldn't be venting his anger on Twitter late at night. I've seen reports in the US media that Trump is increasingly troubled privately. Although the military assured him that the US could successfully seize Kharg Island from Iran, Trump still disagrees because he fears a large number of US soldiers will become "living targets" for Iran. Previously, after a US warplane was shot down by Iran, Trump yelled at his aides for hours. As a result, during the subsequent rescue operation, his aides kept him out of the room, only informing him of significant developments. Reportedly, his aides even asked Trump to give fewer interviews and tweets because many of his statements contradicted each other. Trump initially agreed, but old habits die hard, and he quickly reverted to his old habits, making various impromptu pronouncements late at night and even in the early morning. This led to the various repetitive statements, insults, and dramatic reversals we witnessed. Secondly, the US truly botched some things. Regarding the recent opening and closing of the Strait of Hormuz, the situation could have been very different if the US had reciprocated with a gesture of goodwill from Iran's foreign minister and announced the lifting of its own blockade of the Strait. However, Trump clearly couldn't control his tongue. His pronouncements emphasized America's superiority, even claiming that Iran's opening was unilateral and that the US wouldn't lift the blockade, asserting that he could easily obtain Iran's enriched uranium and that he had complete control. Such humiliation was unacceptable to Iran. Therefore, the re-closing of the Strait of Hormuz is a severe slap in the face to Trump's confident pronouncements. More importantly, this also made Iran realize that it shouldn't overestimate the United States. This further reduced the room for maneuver for moderates in Iran, and a hardline stance became the keynote for Iran's negotiations, leaving the United States with even less room for maneuver. Third, let's wait for more reversals. We shouldn't be too optimistic. This is a group of experienced players; if we're too optimistic, we'll be proven wrong. Of course, we shouldn't be too pessimistic either. Wars always end, and the closer we get to the end, the more unexpected the turn of events might be, and suddenly things will come to a head. Trump will definitely declare victory, and might even rush to Pakistan to sign a peace agreement, announcing to the world that he has ended 10 wars. After all, he started these wars, and he'll end them—in his eyes, this is his great achievement of peace. Iran will also definitely declare victory. Having withstood US pressure, its regime hasn't collapsed; instead, it has received guarantees it hadn't obtained in previous negotiations. For relatively weak Iran, what is this if not a victory? As for the specific terms, it depends on how they are interpreted. With Trump's eloquent rhetoric, he can make the dead seem alive, let alone written terms. The whole world will definitely be stunned. Military and national affairs, war and peace—it all feels like child's play. Sometimes, reality is more absurd than a script—even if a screenwriter dared to write it that way, the audience might not believe it. I saw some foreign netizens joking on social media, saying that although some Western politicians always have a loud voice, there's a Turkish story that goes like this: People asked a dog, "Why are you barking so loudly?" The dog replied, "To scare them." "Then why are you wagging your tail?" "Because I'm scared too." This is indeed a direct quote from a foreigner, not my own words, it's genuine. But is this really a Turkish story?

data.title: A quick overview of keynote speeches at the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival
data.description:
Compiled by: Jinse Finance

From April 20th to 23rd, the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival was grandly opened at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre (5BCDE). Several prominent guests from regulatory agencies, the crypto ecosystem, traditional finance, and cutting-edge technology attended the event and shared their insights, from the convergence of traditional finance and the crypto world, to the collision of AI and Web3, and the real-world breakdown of RWA from concept to scale, collectively defining the evolutionary direction of the next generation of the digital world.

Jinse Finance has compiled the keynote speeches to help readers understand the latest development trends and hot topics in the industry.

Lu Weiding: Deep Integration of Web3 and AI to Jointly Build the Foundation of the Intelligent Economy

On the morning of April 20th, Lu Weiding, Vice Chairman of the All-China Federation of Industry and Commerce and Chairman of Wanxiang Group, delivered a speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival.

Lu Weiding: Deep Integration of Web3 and AI to Jointly Build the Foundation of the Intelligent Economy

On the morning of April 20th, Lu Weiding, Vice Chairman of the All-China Federation of Industry and Commerce and Chairman of Wanxiang Group, delivered a speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival. He stated that Web3 is deeply integrating with AI and embodied intelligence to jointly build the foundation of the intelligent economy. Web3 represents order, establishing trust and ownership of value; AI represents wisdom, enabling data to generate intelligence and ushering in an era of explosive growth in core productivity; embodied intelligence is the carrier, the digital soul, and the truly tangible entity. Therefore, Web3 protocols, AI algorithms, and embodied intelligence together form a multi-interconnected, value-sharing, and autonomously shared system—a system where one begets two, two begets three, and three begets all things—which is precisely the explosive power of Hong Kong's future Web3 ecosystem. Paul Chan: AI and Web3 are accelerating their development; we should seize the opportunities and meet the challenges. On the morning of April 20th, Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan delivered a speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival. He stated that financial institutions are increasingly using digital asset tokenization to improve efficiency, reduce costs, shorten settlement times, and design innovative products for customers, covering more types of assets. From currencies and bonds to real estate and future income, everything is being represented through tokens. These assets are easily liquid and can be divided into smaller, more manageable units, creating a new way to invest, a new way to fund projects, and a new way to share risk. Paul Chan said that digital intelligence is being embedded in the economy, and the rise of AI agents this year is another noteworthy milestone. They can analyze information, make decisions, and execute actions independently, including direct interaction and interaction with other systems. Undoubtedly, the intersection of Web3 and artificial intelligence will be a game-changer. It is foreseeable that in the future, AI agents will analyze information and fully utilize blockchain infrastructure, improving transaction efficiency to a new level. From finance and trade to wealth management, supply chain operations, and logistics, this will create tremendous new opportunities. However, it also raises many questions: First, the relevant infrastructure (such as payments and settlements) must develop in tandem; we cannot wait too long. But here, the challenge is not only about technology; in a global network environment, we also need common standards and close cross-border cooperation so that activities can be recognized, processed, and monitored in a bold and consistent manner across jurisdictions. Second, the human control and security agenda. Artificial intelligence has the ability to make autonomous decisions, which means we must ensure its behavior is predictable, traceable, and open to human interaction. We need clear "guardrails" to ensure that humans are always in control. In this way, abuse, manipulation, or computational errors can be detected and stopped in time. Third, regulation and accountability. As AI-driven activities span numerous platforms, products, and markets, we must ensure that regulatory and governance frameworks keep pace to properly protect investors, users, and consumers. With the continuous development of AI, we must also address risks such as cybersecurity, fraud, and systemic bias in a coordinated manner. Most importantly, decentralization and digital intelligence do not mean weakened accountability or lowered standards. On the contrary, they can facilitate smarter ways to build compliance and oversight mechanisms to integrate into the financial system, thereby strengthening its overall integrity. Regarding Hong Kong's regulatory policies and stance on Web3 and AI, Paul Chan stated that, as an international financial center, Hong Kong's innovative technologies, tokenization, and AI are now crucial building blocks that will support the future development of mainstream finance. Firstly, Hong Kong maintains a balanced and forward-looking system, guided by the principle of equal activity. The same risks, the same regulations, with a focus on risk management and investor protection. Second, Hong Kong believes the true value of blockchain technology and digital assets lies in solving real-world problems, reducing costs, increasing transparency, and making financial services more inclusive. Third, Hong Kong firmly supports innovation. In fact, Hong Kong regulators bear a dual mission: to conduct prudent regulation and to promote market development. They work hand-in-hand with innovators, providing sandbox environments and pilot projects to test new tools in controlled environments, identify risks, and provide timely and practical feedback. These principles are crucial to our approach—the digital asset approach. One example is stablecoins, which have enormous potential to address long-standing pain points in economic activity, particularly in cross-border trade and payments. At the same time, it is imperative to resolutely protect users and the broader community, while keeping the stability of the financial system in mind. This is why Hong Kong has adopted a steady and gradual approach and has built a strong compliance culture and capabilities. Paul Chan concluded by saying that looking ahead, Hong Kong still has much work to do, requiring encouragement and support for more innovative applications, while continuously learning from experience and refining the framework to keep pace with changes. By maintaining an open and balanced attitude, Hong Kong will become a future hub where next-generation technologies will be researched, applied, and promoted. Chan Ho-lim: Hong Kong is building a comprehensive digital asset regulatory framework, aiming to submit it to the Legislative Council this year. On the morning of April 20, Chan Ho-lim, Under Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury of Hong Kong, delivered a speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival. He pointed out that Hong Kong has developed into an international financial center ranking third in the Global Financial Centres Index and first in Asia, providing a solid foundation for Web3 innovation. Chan Ho-lim stated that the Digital Asset Development Policy Declaration 2.0, released last year, proposed the LEAP framework, covering four pillars: optimized legal regulation (L), expanded tokenized product types (E), promoted application scenarios and cross-sectoral cooperation (A), and talent and partner development (P). Chan revealed that Hong Kong currently has over 10 licensed trading platforms, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority issued its first two stablecoin issuer licenses this month, and the Hong Kong SAR government issued the third batch of tokenized green bonds last year, the largest batch globally. Furthermore, Hong Kong is actively developing a regulatory system for digital asset trading service providers and custody service providers, aiming to submit a draft bill to the Legislative Council within the year. Ip Chi-hang: The work for the coming year will be divided into three major themes; a framework for tokenized asset trading on virtual asset trading platforms will be announced. On the morning of April 20th, Ip Chi-hang, Executive Director of the Intermediaries Division of the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC), delivered a keynote speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival. He reviewed six major milestones since the SFC launched the ASPIRe roadmap last year, including allowing licensed platforms to provide staking services, conducting joint consultations on virtual asset trading and custody systems, opening up perpetual contracts and margin financing frameworks, and launching a plan to strengthen market defenses through technology. Ip Chi-hang stated that the draft ordinance for the four systems of virtual asset trading, custody, management, and advisory services has reached 260 pages and was received last week. He mentioned that the work plan for the coming year will be divided into three clusters: First, promoting innovation through regulation, advancing legislation and regulatory guidance consultation; second, promoting innovation through practice, gradually allowing tokenized authorized funds to trade on licensed platforms; and third, promoting innovation through interaction, advancing automated reporting, signing international bilateral memorandums of understanding, and establishing a framework to combat financial crime. In his speech, Ye Zhiheng also stated that on the afternoon of April 20th, he will announce a framework allowing virtual asset trading platforms to trade tokenized assets, not limited to tokenized money market funds, but including all authorized assets. He stated that this is effectively establishing the first tokenized asset trading framework globally, allowing it to be conducted on regulated platforms. Therefore, the experiment will begin with money market funds, and will then be gradually expanded to all authorized assets. Furthermore, issues such as the structural design of perpetual contracts, financing guarantees, and capital adequacy ratios are under in-depth discussion.

Chiu Tat-kan: Hong Kong Needs a Stable, Transparent, and Continuous Policy and Development Environment

On the morning of April 20th, Hong Kong Legislative Council member Chiu Tat-kan delivered a keynote speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival. He stated that **regulatory clarity is the primary condition for industry development**. Comparing the regulatory paths of the United States and Hong Kong, Chiu Tat-kan pointed out that although the United States has introduced important documents such as the Clarity Act, the Genius Act, and the joint statement between the SEC and the CFTC, the political situation may lead to legislative interruptions or even reversals; in contrast, **Hong Kong provides a stable, transparent, and continuous development environment in terms of product classification, legislation, and infrastructure, which are also necessary for the development of Web3 in Hong Kong**.

Chiu Tat-kan: Hong Kong Needs a Stable, Transparent, and Continuous Policy and Development Environment

Regarding the potential threat of quantum computing to the cryptography industry, Chiu Tat-kan stated that he believes it will take at least five more years, and that it is still too early to talk about quantum computing. However, the industry should prepare at the cryptographic level in advance. Chiu emphasized that Hong Kong needs more technology developers and should promote inter-jurisdictional connectivity to create a positive flywheel of product launches, investor participation, and innovator entry. Chiu concluded by saying that the next step is to launch more products to the market, clearly define how these products will be approved, what the approval process will be, what market participants can buy, how to respond to technological innovation, and how to make the market accept it.

data.title: Justin Sun Calls for Settlement After KelpDAO Hackers Exploit Cross-Chain Flaw and Hit Aave With Losses
data.description:
A single exploit has pushed two major DeFi platforms into crisis, exposing how quickly trust can evaporate when cross-chain systems fail.

KelpDAO Bridge Breach Sparks Record Defi Loss

An attacker drained 116,500 rsETH, worth roughly $292 million, from KelpDAO’s cross-chain bridge on 18 April 2026, marking the largest DeFi exploit of the year.

The breach stemmed from a flaw in its LayerZero-powered bridge, where forged cross-chain messages allowed the hacker to mint rsETH without burning tokens on the source chain.

KelpDAO quickly paused its contracts across mainnet and multiple Layer 2 networks, while working with partners and auditors to trace the root cause.

Earlier today we identified suspicious cross-chain activity involving rsETH. We have paused rsETH contracts across mainnet and several L2s while we investigate.

We are working with @LayerZero_Core, @unichain, our auditors and top security experts on RCA.

We will keep you…

— Kelp (@KelpDAO) April 18, 2026

Early findings point to a possible private key leak or a deeper issue tied to its single-validator bridge setup.

How Did The Attack Spill Into Aave

The stolen rsETH did not remain idle. The attacker deposited the tokens into Aave V3 as collateral and borrowed large amounts of Wrapped Ether.

Because the rsETH backing was effectively fake, the positions became unliquidatable.

This left Aave carrying more than $236 million in bad debt, despite its own smart contracts not being compromised.

Today's @KelpDAO exploiter deposited the stolen $rsETH into various lending protocols (AaveV3, CompoundV3, Euler) and and borrowed massive $WETHs w/ >$236m debt. https://t.co/nudJs9cifVpic.twitter.com/94uDBXYSDb

— PeckShieldAlert (@PeckShieldAlert) April 18, 2026

Aave responded by freezing rsETH markets on both V3 and V4 within hours.

Its founder confirmed the issue originated outside the protocol, but the damage had already spread.

rsETH has been frozen on Aave V3 and V4, the asset does not have any borrowing power as a measure due to KelpDAO bridge exploit that happened outside of Aave. Both Aave V3 and V4 does not have further exposure to rsETH. https://t.co/vt8j1BOUjB

— Stani (@StaniKulechov) April 18, 2026

Liquidity Crunch Triggers $5.4 Billion Exit

Fear moved faster than any official response.

Users rushed to withdraw funds, pushing Aave’s ETH utilisation rate to 100%, meaning all available ETH in the pool had been borrowed.

More than $5.4 billion worth of ETH exited the platform as large holders acted swiftly to avoid being trapped.

Once liquidity dried up, remaining depositors faced delays, unable to withdraw until loans are repaid or fresh capital enters.

🚨 Aave just got hit hard after a vulnerability tied to rsETH shook confidence in the system. What started as an exploit through Kelp DAO spiraled into bad debt pressure and then pure panic.

Over 5.4 billion dollars worth of ETH reportedly flowed out as big players rushed for… pic.twitter.com/vnZGNj4s9H

— Solix Trading (@Solix_Trade) April 19, 2026

The situation also hit Aave’s market metrics, with its valuation and total value locked dropping sharply over the weekend.

Justin Sun Steps In And Pulls Funds

Among the largest movers was Justin Sun, who withdrew 65,584 ETH, valued at about $154 million, from Aave and redirected it to other platforms.

His exposure to Aave reportedly fell to $380 million, while his positions in Spark and related platforms climbed to $2.13 billion.

Sun also took an unusual approach by directly addressing the attacker, writing:

“OK — Kelpdao hacker, how much you want? Let’s just talk. With KelpDAO’s help, of course. It’s simply not worth it to sacrifice both Aave and KelpDAO and let them go down over this hack.”

OK — Kelpdao hacker, how much you want? Let’s just talk. With KelpDAO’s help, of course. It’s simply not worth it to sacrifice both Aave and KelpDAO and let them go down over this hack. You can’t spend $300 million anyway.

— H.E. Justin Sun 👨‍🚀 🌞 (@justinsuntron) April 19, 2026

Can The Hacker Be Forced To Return Funds

Sun’s public appeal reflects a familiar playbook in crypto incidents, where negotiation is sometimes used to recover stolen assets.

He added that moving such a large sum would be difficult, noting:

“You can’t spend $300 million anyway.”

Other industry voices echoed the sentiment, pointing out that the transparency of blockchain transactions makes laundering funds at this scale highly challenging.

Was A Single Point Of Failure To Blame

Attention has turned to the bridge’s design.

Interoperability protocol Axelar highlighted the risks of relying on a single validator, suggesting that stronger multi-validator systems could reduce such vulnerabilities.

Every hacking and stolen money incident harms the trust users place on our blockchain systems as a whole and delays adoption of the global worldwide ledger we're envisioning. We stand with the @LayerZero_Core team as they're navigating this difficult situation and rebuilding…

— Axelar Network (@axelar) April 19, 2026

Investigators are now examining whether the exploit originated from a compromised source-chain key or a failure within the verification network. Both scenarios point to structural weaknesses in cross-chain infrastructure.

What Happens To Aave’s Bad Debt Problem

The immediate concern is how Aave handles the unbacked rsETH positions.

If the funds cannot be recovered, the protocol must decide how to absorb or distribute losses, a process that can be complex and contentious.

Until liquidity returns, the platform remains under pressure, with users watching closely for updates.

Meanwhile, KelpDAO’s contracts stay paused as forensic analysis continues, with a full breakdown of the exploit still pending.

data.title: a16z: Intelligent agents begin to manipulate real systems. Who is the actual controller?
data.description:
Authors: Christian Catalini, Andy Hall, Noah Levine, Christian Crowley; Source: a16z crypto; Compiled by: Shaw Jinse Finance

AI agents have rapidly evolved from auxiliary assistants into major economic participants, developing at a pace far exceeding that of surrounding supporting infrastructure.

While AI agents can currently perform tasks and complete transactions, in cross-environment scenarios, they still lack standardized methods to prove their identity, operational permissions, and the path to obtain rewards. Identity cannot be transferred, payments lack programmability by default, and collaborative operations remain isolated.

Blockchain solves these problems at the infrastructure level. Public ledgers provide auditable credentials for every transaction, wallets grant agents transferable identities, and stablecoins offer an optional settlement layer. These are not foundational technologies of the future—they are ready for immediate use, enabling agents to operate autonomously and without permission as genuine economic participants. This article will explain how blockchain bridges these gaps in four dimensions: identity, payment, governance, and trust.

1. Non-human Agent Identity System

The bottleneck of the intelligent agent economy now lies in the identity system, not the level of intelligence..

In the financial services industry alone, the number of non-human agents—automated transaction systems, risk engines, and anti-fraud models—is already about 100 times that of human employees. And with the large-scale deployment of modern intelligent agent frameworks (large language models that can call tools, autonomous workflows, and multi-agent collaborative scheduling), this proportion will continue to rise across industries.

However, these intelligent agents are still essentially in a "bankless" state. They can access financial systems, but their interaction methods lack portability, verifiability, and default trust. They lack standardized mechanisms to prove their operational authority, operate independently across platforms, or take responsibility for their actions. What the industry currently lacks is a universal identity layer—equivalent to an SSL security protocol in the intelligent agent field—to standardize cross-platform collaboration processes. Although many well-known solutions have attempted to address this issue, the paths remain fragmented: on one side is a vertically integrated, fiat-centric technical architecture; on the other side are cryptographically native open standards (such as the x402 protocol and emerging intelligent agent identity proposals); and there are also developer framework extensions like the Model Context Protocol (MCP) that attempt to establish an application-layer identity system. To date, there is still no widely adopted, interoperable solution that allows one agent to prove to another who it represents, what it is allowed to do, and how it receives compensation. This is precisely the core concept of KYA (Know Your Agent). Just as humans rely on credit records and KYC (Know Your Customer) mechanisms, agents also need cryptographically signed credentials to bind them to their delegators, operational permissions, constraints, and reputation. Blockchain provides a neutral collaboration layer for this: transferable identities, programmable wallets, and verifiable claims that can be used across chat applications, APIs, and marketplaces. Early implementations have emerged in the industry: on-chain agent registries, agents based on native wallets (using USDC for settlement), the ERC standard for "least trusted agents," and developer toolkits that combine identity systems with embedded payments and anti-fraud controls. However, before a universal identity standard is introduced, merchants will still block intelligent agents from accessing the system at the firewall level. 2. Governance of AI-Driven Systems Intelligent agents are beginning to manipulate real systems, which raises a series of new questions: Who is the actual controller? Imagine a community or enterprise where an AI system coordinates core resources—whether it's financial allocation or supply chain management. Even if humans have the right to vote on policy changes, if the underlying AI layer is controlled by a single service provider, which can arbitrarily push model updates, adjust constraints, or overturn decisions, then human voice will become extremely weak. The governance layer in form may be decentralized, but the actual operational layer remains centralized—whoever controls the model ultimately controls the outcome. When intelligent agents assume governance functions, a new layer of dependency is introduced. Theoretically, this could make direct democracy more feasible: everyone could have an AI representative who interprets lengthy proposals, models and weighs pros and cons, and votes according to the explicit preferences of the principal. However, this vision hinges on these intelligent agents being truly accountable to the people they represent, being transferable across service providers, and technically constrained to obey human instructions. Otherwise, it will ultimately result in a system that appears democratic but is actually dominated by the behavior of a black-box model that is uncontrollable by humans. If the current reality is that intelligent agents are built from only a few basic models, we need a mechanism to prove that the agents are acting in the interests of the users, not the interests of the model companies. This will likely require providing cryptographic safeguards on multiple levels: Clearly defining the specific training data, fine-tuning methods, or reinforcement learning processes from which a model instance originates; Clearly defining the precise prompts and instructions governing a particular intelligent agent; Retaining all records of its behavior in the real world; Providing a reliable commitment that, after deployment, the service provider cannot arbitrarily modify the instructions or retrain the model without the user's knowledge. Without these safeguards, governance implemented by intelligent agents will ultimately degenerate into governance implemented by the party controlling the model's weights. This is precisely where cryptography can play a crucial role. If collective decisions are recorded on-chain and executed automatically, AI systems must adhere to verified results; if agents possess cryptographic identities and transparent execution logs, their representatives can be verified to ensure they are acting within authorized limits; if the AI ​​layer is user-owned and portable, rather than tied to a single platform, no single company can arbitrarily change the rules by updating the model. Ultimately, governing AI systems is fundamentally an infrastructure issue, not a policy issue. True power depends on building enforceable and trustworthy safeguards within the system.

3. Filling the Gaps in the Traditional Payment System for AI-Native Businesses

AI agents have begun to autonomously engage in consumption—for example, web scraping, browser sessions, and image generation—and stablecoins are gradually becoming an alternative settlement layer for these transactions. At the same time, a new type of transaction market oriented towards intelligent agents is taking shape. Take Stripe and Tempo's MPP marketplace as an example. This platform aggregates over 60 services designed specifically for AI agents, processing over 34,000 transactions in its first week with fees as low as $0.003. Stablecoins are also listed as one of the default payment methods. The core difference lies in how these services are accessed: none of them have a checkout page. The agent reads the data structure, sends a request, completes the payment, and receives the result—the entire process is completed in a single interaction. They represent a new type of "headless merchant"—consisting only of a server, a set of interface endpoints, and a single-call pricing mechanism, without any front-end interface, online store, or sales team. The payment infrastructure supporting all of this is now operational. Coinbase's x402 protocol uses a different technical path than MPP, but both embed payment functionality directly into HTTP requests. Visa is also expanding its card payment network in a similar direction through a command-line tool, allowing developers to initiate payments directly on the terminal, while merchants receive stablecoin settlements instantly in the backend. Related data is still in its early stages. After excluding fraudulent transactions such as fake transactions, x402 currently processes approximately $1.6 million in agent-driven payments per month, far lower than the $24 million reported by Bloomberg recently, citing data from x402.org. However, its surrounding infrastructure is expanding rapidly: Stripe, Cloudflare, Vercel, and Google have all integrated x402 into their platforms. Developer tools represent a significant opportunity, with vibe coding expanding the boundaries of the software development community and thus increasing the overall potential market size for development tools. Companies like Merit Systems are building the infrastructure for this trend with AgentCash, a command-line wallet and marketplace platform that integrates with both MPP and x402 protocols. These products allow agents to use stablecoins in a unified account balance to purchase the data, tools, and capabilities they need. For example, a sales team's AI agent can refine sales lead information by calling a single interface endpoint, combining data from Apollo, Google Maps, and Whitepages, all without the user leaving the command-line interface. Besides emerging bank card-based solutions, there are several reasons why these AI agents tend to use encrypted payment channels for commercial transactions. Firstly, risk control and credit granting. Payment service providers need to assume the corresponding business risks when integrating merchants. For headless merchants without independent websites or legal entities, traditional payment service providers find it difficult to complete risk assessment and credit granting. Secondly, stablecoins enable permissionless programmability in open networks: any developer can set up payment functionality for the interface endpoint without needing to connect to a payment processor or sign a merchant agreement. We have seen similar industry trends before. Every change in business transaction models gives rise to a new type of merchant that is initially difficult for existing systems to adapt to. Companies building this kind of infrastructure are not betting on the current monthly transaction volume of $1.6 million, but on the market size that can be achieved when intelligent agents become the mainstream transacting parties. 4. Reconstructing Trust Pricing in the Intelligent Agent Economy For 300,000 years, human cognition has been the core bottleneck restricting development. Now, AI is pushing the marginal cost of execution to zero. When a scarce resource becomes abundant, the constraint shifts. When intelligence becomes cheap, what becomes expensive? Validation. In the intelligent agent economy, the real limitation to scalability lies in our biologically limited ability to audit and assess the risks of machine decisions. The processing power of intelligent agents has long surpassed the regulatory limits of humans. Due to the high cost of regulation and the lag in risk outbreaks, the market naturally tends to underinvest in this area. "Humans in the loop" is rapidly becoming physically infeasible. However, deploying unverified intelligent agents brings cumulative risks. Systems can coldly optimize around "alternative metrics," silently deviating from human intent, creating a false appearance of productivity, and masking the accumulation of massive AI debt. To safely entrust the economy to machines, trust can no longer rely on human verification—trust must be hard-coded into the system architecture itself. When anyone can generate content for free, the most important thing is verifiable origin—knowing where it comes from and whether it is trustworthy. Blockchain, along with on-chain credentials and decentralized digital identity systems, expands the economic boundaries for secure AI deployment. We no longer need to treat AI as a black box, but can obtain a clear and auditable history of its behavior. As more AI agents begin to transact with each other, settlement networks and traceability of origin become increasingly intertwined. Systems that handle funds—such as stablecoins and smart contracts—can also carry cryptographic credentials, recording who did what and who is responsible when problems arise. Human comparative advantage will shift upwards: from correcting minor errors to setting strategic directions and taking responsibility when systems fail. Sustainable competitive advantage belongs to those entities that can provide cryptographic proof of their outputs, insure them, and take responsibility for failures. Unverified scaling is a liability that accumulates over time. 5. Ensuring User Control

5. Ensuring User Control

For decades, layers of abstraction have defined how users interact with technology. Programming languages ​​abstracted machine code, command lines were replaced by graphical user interfaces, and then evolved into mobile applications and APIs. Each change has hidden more underlying complexity while always keeping users firmly in control.

In an agent-driven world, users only specify the target result, not the specific steps, and the system autonomously determines the implementation path. Intelligent agents are not merely abstract methods of task execution, but also abstract the subjects that perform those tasks. After setting initial parameters, users can relinquish control, and the system will operate autonomously. The user's role shifts from direct interaction to supervision and management; without user intervention, the system defaults to continuous operation. As users delegate more and more tasks to intelligent agents, new risks emerge: **Ambiguous input may cause the agent to act based on incorrect assumptions without the user's awareness; malfunctions may be concealed, making it impossible to clearly troubleshoot problems; a simple authorization may trigger unexpected, multi-step, complex processes.** This is precisely where encryption technology comes into play. The core of encryption technology is always to minimize blind trust. When users delegate more decision-making power to software, intelligent agent systems exacerbate this problem and place higher demands on design rigor—through setting clearer permission boundaries, increasing transparency, and providing stronger constraints on system behavior. A new generation of native encryption tools has emerged to address this need. Delegation frameworks with defined scopes, such as the MetaMask delegation toolkit, Coinbase AgentKit and smart agent wallets, and Merit Systems' AgentCash, allow users to define the boundaries of smart agent permissions at the smart contract level. Intent-based architectures, such as NEAR Intents (which has processed over $15 billion in decentralized exchange trading volume since Q4 2024), allow users to simply set a goal, such as "cross-chain tokens and staking," without specifying concrete execution steps. A new internet infrastructure where smart agents directly participate in economic activities is under construction. The unresolved question is: will this system be designed with extreme transparency, accountability, and user control as its guiding principles, or will it be built on a system not originally designed for non-human entities?

data.title: Galaxy: API Payment in the Era of Contactless SaaS and Artificial Intelligence
data.description:
Author: Vikram Singh, Source: Galaxy, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Overview

  • Today's software payment system is built for static, manually driven partnerships. For new developers who have never written a single line of code, configuring and managing APIs/MCPs is extremely cumbersome. High-quality data sources are constantly losing customers because large language models cannot dynamically interact with paywalls.

The Development History of SaaS to Date

The past few weeks have seen noteworthy industry developments from the perspective of smart agent payments: Tempo launched its mainnet, with the Machine Payment Protocol (MPP) performing particularly well; Ramp launched smart agent cards last week; Visa released command-line tools and is actively promoting its smart payment business; Coinbase has also been leading the development of the x402 protocol in recent months.

However, the industry focus is currently mostly on commercial shopping scenarios within Large Language Model (LLM) chat interfaces, with very limited discussion on software and data payments. I believe that AI will fundamentally change our future understanding of software payments and the SaaS model, and this article will explore this viewpoint.

The first software licensing agreement was signed in 1969 when IBM decided to split its large and expensive hardware and software services for sale.

Years later, the software finance sector is now dominated by the SaaS model, which can be further divided into high-involvement (e.g., Salesforce, SAP) and low-involvement (e.g., X, Stripe). High-involvement SaaS has a high average order value, high customer acquisition cost per new user, and high customer support costs; low-involvement SaaS has a lower average order value and lower customer acquisition cost per new user, but overall customer acquisition investment remains high to acquire a large number of users. With the unstoppable development of AI, the software payment system is reaching a turning point, potentially giving rise to the contactless SaaS model I define. The gradually disappearing programming barriers and the birth of contactless SaaS: Programming tools have lowered the barrier to entry and will continue to do so, covering a wider range of "developers" with limited programming experience. We can fully foresee that within just a few years, programming experience will no longer be a barrier to software development—in fact, this trend is already evident. Currently, the minor obstacles to developing personal or non-enterprise software (aside from a small amount of operational knowledge) mainly focus on: finding suitable data sources, tools, and APIs; configuring API keys and completing service payments; and integrating them into one's own application. The third point has been abstracted and simplified by tools such as Claude Code and Codex, while the first two points still have significant room for innovation and optimization (to some extent, machine payment protocols have already brought about related changes). The current process of configuring API keys is cumbersome and inefficient: ① Find the corresponding API → ② Create an account → ③ Complete identity verification via email verification code → ④ Add a payment method → ​​⑤ Generate an API key → ⑥ Store the key → ⑦ Configure call rate limits → ⑧ Set up billing reminders → ⑨ Write code. This process cannot be skipped, and it needs to be repeated for each new API, tool, or data source. The process of establishing a machine payment protocol connection is similar. For high-value integration scenarios like Stripe and X, the cognitive cost of API selection and the resistance of the configuration process can be amortized through hundreds of thousands of API calls, so this cumbersome process is acceptable; however, for low-frequency usage scenarios, this process is too redundant.

Source: Galaxy Ventures

Leaving aside the mindset of skeuomorphic design, let's think about what the discovery and retrieval of contextual data sources should look like. Suppose I ask my large language model, "What happened in New York today?" It should be able to find the New York Times API, access the relevant API endpoints, and retrieve the relevant news articles for me. Such a one-off API call should not require me to create a Machine Payment Protocol (MCP) connection for a trivial request. Similarly, if I were to posed a broader query: “Write an investment analysis report for stock {ABC}. Consult SEC filings (using the Financial Datasets API), latest news (using the Parallel Web Systems API), and social sentiment (Twitter/X API).” This would involve 3 MCP connections, thus requiring 3 bilateral billing relationships to be established for a single query. This user experience (UX) is terrible and can be improved through aggregation and centralization. Beyond contextual data sources and our high-level large language model workflow, let’s consider how the experience for non-enterprise developers will evolve. Suppose you’ve developed a web application that provides the above stock analysis workflow and want to roll it out to a user base of 2,500 customers—not enterprise-scale, but certainly not small. To ensure your application runs stably and without crashing in a production environment, you might need to upgrade your services like Financial Datasets, Parallel, and Twitter/X to higher-priced plans, causing your server costs (Vercel, Supabase, Railway, etc.) to skyrocket. This means you've accumulated (and locked in) significant costs to provide the service. At this point, you have the following options: Pay the high upfront costs yourself, but the revenue stream is uncertain; Seek venture capital, but your semi-formal, amateur project is unlikely to attract funding; Have your customers pay based on real-time usage. The cost calculation method for real-time payment can be: User bundling cost = Cumulative API cost (Financial Datasets, Parallel, Twitter/X) + Cumulative server cost (Vercel, Supabase, Railway) + Value-added/Platform service fee. Real-time payment can theoretically solve the cold start problem that requires prepayment and supports dynamic pricing models (adjusting fees based on supply and demand, and optimizing pricing packages and server space based on real-time usage). Content, Data, and Aggregation Let's look at two cases: Case 1 "In-depth research into the US GPU and data center market. Who are the most important players? What are the chip manufacturers' import and export volumes? Where are new data centers being built?" This type of search depth is structurally limited, and the results are often mediocre. It can only access the free version of SemiAnalysis and other free data sources, while truly high-quality data is hidden behind paywalls. Case Study 2: Using the same prompt as Case Study 1 + "This research is important to me. You have a budget of $10, please use the best data source." Source: Galaxy Ventures Assuming the data source supports pay-as-you-go API endpoints, the large language model can autonomously retrieve these data sources, complete payments using its budget, and leverage high-value information to refine in-depth research. To achieve the same effect as Case 2 in Case 1, human users must manually find data sources, configure keys, and establish billing relationships with various API/MCP service providers. In Case 2, the large language model dynamically retrieves and pays for the optimal data source, saving humans the costs of searching, decision-making, and repetitive configuration. As a side note, but crucially: the above examples reveal a fundamental misalignment in the data and contextual data source discovery process. The exploration costs of finding suitable APIs and the decision-making costs of determining whether an API is worth paying for are shifted from the end user to the large language model's retrieval engine and budget constraints. From this perspective, the large language model is becoming the core aggregation layer for content and data. Why hasn't AI budgeting become widespread yet? Implementing AI budgets requires calling APIs while the large language model is running, and runtime API calls demand atomicity in payments. For example, if you pay $5 to call the XYZ API for a task, how can you guarantee that XYZ will actually provide the service and not simply take the money? If payments and data acquisition can be done atomically, we can have a trustless system. To scale this, we need micropayments. However, the existing financial infrastructure is structurally incapable of supporting micropayments. Credit cards charge approximately 2.9% + $0.30 in fees per transaction. Assuming a call cost of $0.01, the fixed fees alone are 30 times the API cost. Based on these rates, the minimum feasible transaction amount with fees less than 10% of the cost is $4.29. However, credit cards can serve as an abstraction layer for micropayments (explained in detail later). Therefore, pay-per-API pricing is inherently unsuitable for existing payment channels. The industry can only solve API payment issues through bulk billing: pre-deposited funds, monthly bills, and subscription packages. You won't see 100,000 individual $0.001 transactions in a Claude Code call; Anthropic will only deduct $100 from your pre-deposited account. Similarly, Amazon doesn't bill each AWS Lambda call individually, but instead issues a single monthly bill. This model works, so why change it? First, in both scenarios, you handle the retrieval process yourself, cost planning is your (or your company's) decision-making burden, and the architecture design of the call method is also determined by you. Each new API requires establishing a new static bilateral partnership. Each billing cycle involves pre-paying for the services expected to be used. In the world of dynamic retrieval, establishing static partnerships is a major bottleneck. Prepaying funds is meaningless when you don't even know which API you'll use. This necessitates a new infrastructure: one that can establish partnerships without breaking trust, and one that can facilitate payments free from the constraints of outdated pricing systems. A natural solution is blockchain-based micropayments. But have micropayments never succeeded historically? Digicash, Flooz, Beenz, and Blendle are all failures in the micropayment field. However, as Nick Szabo argues in his article "The Psychological Barriers to Micropayments," micropayments were destined to fail from the start—the cost of human decision-making and record-keeping is usually higher than the actual transaction cost. Simply put, humans value predictability more than accuracy. A $15 monthly Spotify subscription, even if only half-used, is psychologically superior to a pay-as-you-go model that might ultimately cost only $7.50. The former has lower psychological transaction costs, while the latter requires multiple thought processes. This is why subscriptions win: they transform a series of small decisions with a fixed average value into a single, predictable large decision (which allows service providers to charge more than the actual usage value). This is also why "waste" is common in SaaS pricing—users often pay more than they actually use. However, this logic no longer holds true when economic agents shift from humans to large language models/AI agents. AI agents do not experience decision fatigue; their "psychological accounting costs" are not measured by cognitive effort but by reasoning computational power. The only constraint on the agent is the budget allocated by humans, which can be simplified to a simple cost minimization function. Achieving Contactless SaaS: Low-intervention SaaS reduces customer acquisition costs by eliminating sales teams. Contactless SaaS, on the other hand, allows tools and data sources to be directly retrieved and atomically paid, completely eliminating the customer acquisition process. This requires three infrastructure changes: Machine-readable price discovery: This is equivalent to forming an API marketplace. It can be understood as adding a layer of request headers or metadata containing standardized pricing information on top of an MCP that already has the ability to describe functions. Skills.sh leaderboards and plugin marketplaces have already shown this prototype.

  • Payment and API Abstraction: Currently, "one-click retrieval of paid data" remains largely theoretical, as most services still require the creation of API keys. MCP achieves abstraction to some extent, with Coinbase's x402 protocol and Tempo's Machine Payment Protocol (MPP) being particularly valuable in this area.

  • Instant Settlement: Due to structural requirements, settlement must be fast and low-cost. It is well known that the speed of fund transfers is much slower than that of software. This is usually not a bottleneck, but in this scenario, ultra-fast payments are crucial. If the payment completion speed is slower than the data return speed, the service provider bears the credit risk brought by the intelligent agent, thus requiring intermediary intervention. Atomicity requirements set a lower limit for data retrieval latency, and this lower limit is determined by the execution speed of the underlying payment channel.

    • Discussion on the Economic Model of Contactless SaaS

    As Patrick McKenzie (@patio11) of Stripe stated, the most basic revenue formula for SaaS is:

    Revenue = Σ(Customer Acquisition × Conversion Rate × Average Revenue per User) / Churn Rate

    High-involvement SaaS maximizes average revenue per user through six-figure transaction amounts per customer. Low-involvement SaaS maximizes customer acquisition and conversion efficiency through product-driven growth, freemium models, and low barriers to entry.

    In a contactless model, customer acquisition costs theoretically approach zero—unless there are methods like bribing agents or paying to improve API rankings—because agents automatically search for resources.

    The conversion rate is also almost 100% because retrieval and payment are atomically completed simultaneously. However, the average revenue per user drops significantly: revenue is no longer the $15 monthly subscription fee, but a single call fee of $0.0X, with extremely volatile call volume. In this model, the unit economics model only holds true when transaction volume scales up. A service with a single call fee of $0.003 requires 5,000 calls per month to break even with the $15 subscription fee. There are still many issues in this area worth exploring, which I will discuss in subsequent articles. Finally, some macro-level thoughts and open questions: With instant onboarding and higher searchability, service providers can serve millions of intelligent agents simultaneously, rather than thousands of human subscribers. Therefore, even if revenue per user decreases, the number of "users" may grow exponentially. Contactless SaaS requires participants to accept a low-margin, high-transaction-volume model. Currently, is the appeal of dynamic retrieval using large language models sufficient to persuade enterprises to shift from subscription-based to pay-as-you-go pricing? To achieve large-scale real-time pay-as-you-go pricing, the cost of the underlying payment channel must be extremely low to avoid eroding already meager profits. How should value be distributed across different layers? Can the large language model aggregation layer (Anthropic/OpenAI) include APIs in its catalog and charge platform fees? What is the profit margin for the financial layer/blockchain (structurally unable to charge high fees)? How should the authorization and real-name authentication process be designed? How to distinguish between genuine large language model API calls and Sybil attacks? What role do credit cards play in this (MPP supports credit cards as a payment method)? How does session-based payment actually work? Who bears the credit risk? Virtual cards are a direction worth paying attention to. A New Payment Paradigm Contactless SaaS reveals a completely new software payment paradigm: the retrieval layer shifts from humans to intelligent agents, and the decision-making cost of service selection shifts from human emotion to a quantified cost-benefit trade-off. Since data acquisition and payment need to be atomically synchronized, real-time payment becomes a necessity. In the next article, I will delve into how these trends reshape API bundling models, how profits are distributed at each level, and what form a fully native intelligent agent economy will actually take in reality.

data.title: Iran plans to enact a law governing the Strait of Hormuz; Vercel discloses security incident.
data.description:
Headline

▌Iran Plans to Introduce Strait of Hormuz Management Law, Banning Israeli-Affiliated Vessels

On April 20th, local time on the 19th, Rezaei Kochi, Chairman of the Civil Engineering Committee of the Iranian Parliament, stated that Iran has drafted a comprehensive law on the management of the Strait of Hormuz, which is nearing finalization. According to this law, the passage of goods and vessels affiliated with the Israeli regime will be prohibited. Belligerent nations will also be prohibited from passing through without permission from Iran's Supreme National Security Council. Furthermore, any country that has caused damage to Iran will be prohibited from passing through the Strait of Hormuz until compensation is paid. Under the plan, vessels from all countries must pay a fee in rials to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. 30% of the toll revenue will be used to strengthen the armed forces, and 70% will be used to improve the lives of the people.

(CCTV) Vercel disclosed a "limited" security incident on April 19th, stating that it had discovered an unauthorized access to some of Vercel's internal systems. An investigation is underway, and incident response experts have been hired to assist with the investigation and remediation. Vercel has notified law enforcement and will provide updates on the investigation's progress. Vercel has identified a limited number of affected customers directly involved; official services remain operational and will continue to be updated with the latest information. As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $73,870.07, a 24-hour change of -2.5%; ETH price is $2,267.67, a 24-hour change of -3.7%; BNB price is $617.40, a 24-hour change of -2.1%. SOL price is $83.55, a 24-hour change of -3.2%; DOGE price is $0.09301, a 24-hour change of -2.0%; XRP price is $1.40, a 24-hour change of -2.8%; TRX price is $0.3295, a 24-hour change of +0.1%. The price of WLFI is $0.07734, a 24-hour change of -2.3%; the price of HYPE is $40.88, a 24-hour change of -7.1%.

Policy

▌Russia Plans to Strictly Regulate Crypto Trading: Unlicensed Operations Could Result in Up to 7 Years of Forced Labor

On April 19, a draft bill published by the Russian State Duma revealed that Russia plans to introduce criminal liability for unlicensed cryptocurrency trading and exchange operations. Organizing digital currency trading activities without the central bank's permission will be considered a crime, and related operators could face up to 7 years of forced labor. Crypto miners who fail to declare industrial mining activities will also be punished. The draft bill also stipulates that individuals who buy or sell crypto assets without a license may also face criminal penalties, including fines (approximately $1,300 to $4,000) or up to 4 years of forced labor; while operators of large exchanges could face fines of up to $13,000 and possible imprisonment for 5 to 7 years. It is understood that if the bill is passed, it could take effect as early as July 1, 2027.

...

▌Iranian Media: Iran Refuses to Participate in Second Round of Iran-US Talks

Golden Finance reported on April 20 that the Islamic Republic News Agency (IRNA) released an English-language message on social media on April 19, stating that Iran refused to participate in the second round of talks with the United States. (Xinhua News Agency)

▌Iran Designates New Shipping Route in Strait of Hormuz; Passage Prohibited Without Permission

On April 20, according to CCTV, local time on April 19, Iranian sources reported that the Iranian Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy had designated a new shipping route from south of Hormuz Island to south of Larak Island. It is understood that this route is named the "Larak Corridor," and passage is prohibited without the permission of the Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy.

... Blockchain Applications ▌EasyDNS Admits Responsibility for eth.limo Domain Hijacking Incident, First Social Engineering Attack in 28 Years On April 20th, ENS web gateway eth.limo released a post-incident analysis report on Saturday, indicating that the DNS hijacking incident that occurred Friday night stemmed from a social engineering attack targeting its domain registrar, EasyDNS. The DNSSEC verification mechanism rejected the attacker's modifications to the name server, and eth.limo stated that no impact on users has been found so far. EasyDNS publicly acknowledged the security incident, stating that this is the first successful social engineering attack against a customer in the registrar's 28-year history. Given the increasing number of cryptocurrency front-end projects being compromised at the DNS layer in recent months, eth.limo will migrate to a more stringent EasyDNS-affiliated service provider that does not support account recovery.

▌Polygon: Polygon Chain, Agglayer, and the Entire Ecosystem Unaffected by the rsETH Vulnerability Event

On April 20th, Polygon posted on the X platform, stating, "The team has been actively monitoring the rsETH vulnerability event: the Polygon chain, Agglayer, and the broader ecosystem, including Katana and Vaultbridge, have not been affected by the event. To date, Polygon has safely transferred over $2 trillion, and we will continue to remain vigilant and monitor the development of the event."

Cryptocurrency

▌$138 Million Liquidated Across the Network in the Past 4 Hours, Primarily Long Positions Liquidated

▌Due to KelpDAO attackers depositing rsETH into Aave to borrow ETH, a large number of whales have begun withdrawing ETH from Aave

On April 19th, according to Lookonchain monitoring, due to the KelpDAO vulnerability incident, attackers deposited rsETH into Aave to borrow ETH, resulting in bad debts on Aave. A large number of whales have begun withdrawing ETH from Aave. Currently, the ETH utilization rate on Aave has risen to 100%.

...

▌TRUMP Token Team Transfers 7.59 Million TRUMP, Worth Approximately $21.81 Million, to Exchanges

On April 19, according to Onchain Lens monitoring, the TruMP token team transferred 7.59 million TRUMP, worth approximately $21.81 million, to the BitGo escrow address. These funds were subsequently transferred to centralized exchanges.

Important Economic Developments

▌Iranian Foreign Ministry Says Unconditional Transit Through the Strait of Hormuz "Is History"

On April 19, Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Bagae said early on the 19th that the unconditional transit through the Strait of Hormuz "became history" the moment the aggression of the United States and Israel brought US military forces into the vicinity of the strait.

On April 19, Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Bagae said that the moment the aggression of the United States and Israel brought US military forces into the vicinity of the strait, unconditional transit through the Strait of Hormuz "became history."

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, the price of gold rises, while the price of Bitcoin plummets. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data. Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin has an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but does not rise with stocks. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from obtaining a safe-haven status: an oversupply of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeated behavioral records (behavioral accumulation).

Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-prone asset," capable of functioning effectively when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold, but rather a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Large whales withdrew ETH from Aave, and Tether froze USDT belonging to the Rhea hackers.
data.description:
Golden Weekly is a weekly blockchain industry summary column launched by Golden Finance, covering key news, mining information, project updates, technological advancements, and other industry dynamics. This article is the news segment of the weekly column, giving you a glimpse into the major events in the blockchain industry this week.

Headlines

▌Due to KelpDAO attackers depositing rsETH into Aave to borrow ETH, a large number of whales have begun withdrawing ETH from Aave

On April 19th, according to Lookonchain monitoring, due to the KelpDAO vulnerability incident, attackers deposited rsETH into Aave to borrow ETH, resulting in bad debts on Aave. A large number of whales have begun withdrawing ETH from Aave. Currently, the ETH utilization rate on Aave has risen to 100%.

▌Tether has frozen 3.29 million USDT in the Rhea Finance hacker's address

On April 17, Tether CEO Paolo Ardoino posted on X that Tether had frozen 3.29 million USDT in the Rhea Finance hacker's address. Paolo Ardoino specifically mentioned that "Tether takes this matter seriously," seemingly implying that Circle had not restricted hackers' use of USDC to transfer illicit funds in several recent incidents, including Drift.

According to Jinse Finance, on April 19th local time, when discussing the progress of negotiations with the United States, Iranian Parliament Speaker Qaribaf stated in an interview with local media that the negotiations have not eliminated the distrust between the two sides, but both sides have gained a more pragmatic understanding of each other. Some issues have been resolved in the negotiations, while others remain unresolved. He believes that the two sides still have a long way to go before reaching a final agreement. This proposal is based on the three-year tokenization pilot program of Depository Trust Companies (DTCs) and is modeled after similar rules previously approved by the SEC for Nasdaq. Cooperation between China and Venezuela is protected by international law and the laws of both countries, and China's legitimate rights and interests in Venezuela must be guaranteed. (The Paper) ▌Central Cyberspace Administration: Strengthening Legislation in Artificial Intelligence, Digital Economy, Blockchain, and Other Fields On April 17, the Central Cyberspace Affairs Commission Office published an article entitled "Building a Rule-of-Law Cyberspace that Benefits the People," which pointed out the need to continuously improve laws and regulations that promote and protect the development of informatization, especially strengthening legislation in areas such as artificial intelligence, digital economy, network infrastructure, satellite internet, and blockchain, striving to ensure that wherever informatization develops, cyber legislation covers, and using the rule of law to safeguard technological innovation and self-reliance in the cyberspace field.
