(Jinshi) On April 15th, quantum computing concept stocks surged, with Arqit Quantum rising over 17%, IonQ rising over 13%, D-Wave Quantum and Quantum Computing rising over 11%, Rigetti Computing rising over 8%, and Oracle rising over 5%. In related news, NVIDIA announced a new member to its open-source model family—the "Ising" quantum artificial intelligence model, targeting the two core bottlenecks of quantum processor calibration and quantum error correction. In a press release, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated, "AI is crucial for making quantum computing truly practical. With Ising, AI will become the control layer of quantum computing, the operating system of quantum machines, transforming fragile qubits into scalable and reliable quantum GPU systems." (Gelonghui)

This acquisition aims to expand its self-custodied crypto capabilities and strengthen its strategy of connecting traditional finance with on-chain infrastructure. Upon completion of the transaction, eToro will integrate its multi-asset platform with Zengo's non-custodial wallet technology to support the development of digital asset use cases such as tokenized assets, prediction markets, and perpetual contracts.

data.title: Analyzing the culprit behind the biggest hacking case of 2026: the "DVN configuration vulnerability"
data.description:
Author: 0x2333; Source: BlockBeats

On April 18, 2026, Kelp DAO's liquidity restaking protocol was attacked, with attackers withdrawing 116,500 rsETH from the cross-chain bridge within a few hours, equivalent to approximately $293 million at the time. The entire process was unusually efficient, from forging cross-chain messages to distributing the stolen funds across three lending protocols—Aave V3, Compound V3, and Euler—to lend out real assets. The attackers withdrew with $236 million worth of WETH that same day. Aave, SparkLend, and Fluid subsequently froze the rsETH market.

This is the largest DeFi attack so far in 2026.

But one thing distinguishes this attack from most hacking incidents.

Kelp DAO's smart contract code has no vulnerabilities. Security researcher @0xQuit, who participated in the investigation, wrote on X, "From what I currently understand, this is a combination of two issues: a 1-of-1 DVN configuration, and the DVN node itself being compromised." LayerZero's official statement also did not mention the contract code, characterizing the problem as an "rsETH vulnerability" rather than a "LayerZero vulnerability." The $293 million isn't in any line of code. It's hidden in a misfilled configuration parameter during deployment. The general logic of DeFi security audits is: find the contract, read the code, find vulnerabilities. This logic works quite smoothly when dealing with code logic vulnerabilities. Tools like Slither and Mythril have mature detection capabilities for known patterns such as reentrancy attacks and integer overflows. LLM, which has been heavily promoted in the last two years for code auditing, also has some capability against business logic vulnerabilities (such as flash loan arbitrage paths). However, two rows in this matrix are red. Configuration layer vulnerabilities are a structural blind spot in tool auditing. The problem with Kelp DAO isn't in the .sol file, but in a parameter written during protocol deployment—the DVN threshold. This parameter determines how many verification nodes a cross-chain message needs to be confirmed by before it's considered legitimate. It doesn't enter the code, Slither's scanning range, or Mythril's symbolic execution path. According to a comparative study by Dreamlab Technologies, Slither and Mythril detected 5/10 and 6/10 vulnerabilities respectively in the tested contracts, but this achievement is based on the premise that "the vulnerabilities are in the code." According to IEEE research, even at the code level, existing tools can only detect 8%-20% of exploitable vulnerabilities. From the perspective of existing auditing paradigms, there is no tool that can "detect whether the DVN threshold is reasonable." To detect this type of configuration risk, what is needed is not a code analyzer, but a specific configuration checklist: "Is the number of DVNs used in the cross-chain protocol ≥ N?", "Is there a minimum threshold requirement?" These kinds of questions currently lack standardized tools and even widely accepted industry standards. Also in the red area is key and node security. @0xQuit's description mentions that DVN nodes were "breached," which falls under the category of operational security (OpSec) and is beyond the detection limits of any static analysis tool. Neither leading auditing firms nor AI scanning tools can predict whether a node operator's private key will be leaked. This attack simultaneously triggered two red areas in the matrix. DVN is LayerZero V2's cross-chain message verification mechanism, short for Decentralized Verifier Network. Its design philosophy is to delegate security decision-making power to the application layer: each protocol accessing LayerZero can choose how many DVN nodes need to simultaneously confirm before allowing a cross-chain message to pass. This "degree of freedom" creates a spectrum. Kelp DAO chose the leftmost end of the spectrum, 1-of-1, requiring only one DVN node for confirmation. This means zero fault tolerance; an attacker only needs to compromise that one node to forge any cross-chain message. In contrast, Apechain, which also integrates with LayerZero but has configured more than two required DVNs, was unaffected in this incident. LayerZero's official statement says "all other applications remain secure," implying that security depends on the configuration chosen. The normal industry recommendation is at least 2-of-3, where an attacker needs to compromise two independent DVN nodes simultaneously to forge messages, increasing fault tolerance to 33%. High-security configurations like 5-of-9 can achieve fault tolerance of up to 55%. The problem is that external observers and users cannot see this configuration. Both are labeled "backed by LayerZero," but the underlying fault tolerance could be 0% or 55%. Both are referred to as DVN in the documentation. Dovey Wan, a seasoned crypto investor who experienced the Anyswap incident, wrote directly on X: "LayerZero's DVN is actually a 1/1 validator…all cross-chain bridges should immediately undergo a comprehensive security review." In August 2022, a vulnerability was discovered in the Nomad cross-chain bridge. Someone copied the first attack transaction, made slight modifications, and found it successful—several hundred addresses subsequently began copying, draining $190 million within hours. Nomad's post-incident analysis stated that the vulnerability originated from "initializing the trusted root to 0x00 during a routine upgrade." This was a configuration error occurring during the deployment phase. Merkle proved that the verification logic was correct, the code itself was not flawed, the problem was an incorrect initialization value. Combined with Nomad, configuration/initialization vulnerabilities have caused approximately $482 million in losses. In the entire history of cross-chain bridge thefts, this category is comparable in scale to key leaks ($Ronin $624 million, Harmony $100 million, Multichain $126 million, totaling approximately $850 million). However, the code auditing industry's product design has never been geared towards this category. The most discussed issues in the industry are still code logic vulnerabilities. Wormhole's $326 million loss due to signature verification bypass and Qubit Finance's $80 million theft due to fraudulent deposits are examples. These cases have complete vulnerability analysis reports, CVE number comparisons, and reproducible PoCs, making them suitable for training and optimizing auditing tools. Configuration layer issues, if not written into the code, are unlikely to enter the production cycle. A noteworthy detail is that the triggering methods of the two configuration-related events are drastically different. Nomad accidentally entered an incorrect initial value during a routine upgrade, a mistake. Kelp DAO's 1-of-1, however, is a proactive configuration choice—the LayerZero protocol does not prohibit this option, and Kelp DAO has not violated any protocol rules. A "compliant" configuration choice and a "mistaken" initial value ultimately point to the same consequence. The attack's execution logic was simple: a forged cross-chain message informed the Ethereum mainnet that "someone on another chain has locked equivalent assets," triggering the mainnet to mint rsETH. The minted rsETH itself had no actual backing, but its on-chain record was "legitimate" and could be accepted as collateral by lending protocols. The attackers then distributed 116,500 rsETH across Aave V3 (Ethereum and Arbitrum), Compound V3, and Euler, lending out a total of over $236 million in real assets. According to multiple reports, Aave V3 alone faced an estimated $177 million in bad debt. Aave's security module, Umbrella, has approximately $50 million in WETH reserves available to absorb bad debts, covering less than 30% of the total. The remainder will be borne by aWETH stakers. This ultimately falls on those who just want to earn a little WETH interest. LayerZero is still conducting a joint investigation with the security incident response organization SEAL Org, stating that they will release a post-incident analysis report with Kelp DAO after obtaining all the information. Kelp DAO stated that they are conducting "proactive remediation." The $293 million vulnerability is not in the code. The phrase "audit passed" does not cover the location of that parameter.
